Page 1

Bearing 315
JAN-FEB 1987
the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club

1986 CALIFORNIA CHAMPIONSHIPS

by Dave Enger

A strong contingent of 13 Puget Sounders travelled to the San Francisco Bay area for the class "A" California Championships meet hosted by the Bay Area Orienteering Club on October 18 and 19. What awaited us were some of the most physically brutal courses I've ever run.

The event was held at Briones Regional Park, located just west of Concord and Walnut Creek, in the hills east of San Francisco Bay. The area consists of steep, dry, half-open hills, typical of BAOC east bay areas. In fact, the area is so steep that the new 1:15,000 scale map uses a 10 meter contour interval rather than the usual 5 meters! The south-facing slopes are generally open, with a covering of dry grasses. The north-facing slopes are generally wooded, with deciduous trees, many "fight" areas, and abundant poison oak.

The courses were short, but this was more than made up for by the sometimes excessive amounts of climb. The blue course had about 500 meters of climb each day, and all but the white and Day 1 yellow courses exceeded the 4% climb limit set by the International Orienteering Federation, with climbs ranging up to 7%. One 700-meter leg on the Day 1 blue course had a 160-meter climb! Some of us experienced some difficulty adjusting to the 10-meter contour interval, which makes reentrants appear less deep and hills appear less steep on the map. The winning times on the advanced courses were around 100 minutes, much longer than the recommended 60-80 minute winning times.

The highlight of the northwest results was the clean sweep of the women's elite class (F21A) by Debbie Newell, Eileen Williams, and Mary Forbes. Other Puget Sound area participants were Rick Breseman, Dave Enger, Bob Forbes, Arnold Kay, Gary McCausland, Ron Pontius, Mike Schuh, Roger Sharp, and Doug and Katie Sprugel. Wayne Patterson from Portland, and Ross Burnett from Vancouver, B.C. also competed. Ross, a member of the Canadian National O' Team, won the Day 1 blue course despite having his shoe blow out at the 5th control!

The BAOC will be hosting the U.S. Long-O and Relay Championships next June on a new map of a redwood forest area west of San Jose. The meet will be the first weekend of a week-long series of orienteering events in California, including the U.S. Orienteering Federation convention and "A" meet in San Diego. Rumor has it that the BAOC Long-O and Relay area is virtually all wooded, has few man-made features, and has steeper and more detailed terrain than Briones! Since long-o courses are about twice as long as regular orienteering courses, the coming BAOC meet could be a real killer. I am planning to go, but I may run a course shorter than blue. I hope I'll have recovered from the Briones poison oak by then!

Page 2

KNUT OLSON WINS M60 NATIONAL CHAMPIONSHIP

Knut Olson became COC's first national champion with his victory in the men's 60 and over division at the U.S. Orienteering Championships November 1-2 in Texas. Knut, one of Sweden's first orienteers, has lived in this country for over 35 years, and returned to the sport just a couple of years ago. Congratulations, Knut!

CASCADE CHATTER

compiled by Harvey "National Enquirer" Friedman

This column is back by popular demand (at least 5 people asked me to do it), so here it is again.

Special thanks to those half-dozen or so folks who volunteered space to lodge visiting orienteers during the Trollhaugen meet in September. we had space for 50 more people, but those who I didn't get back to weren't needed for that meet. Please step forward to offer your hospitality the next time a call for lodging volunteers is made... COC will be losing two active members soon. Don Denbo will be a research assistant professor in oceanography at Harvard University in Boston beginning in February, and Scott Coble will be a professor in mathematics and computer science at Gonzaga University in Spokane beginning in January. Don will be able to join the biggest club in the U.S., the New England Orienteering Club, where they put on two class "A" meets each year. Some of us will see him at the 1987 U.S. Championships in Rhode Island next October if not before. Scott will probably get to some COC meets, but not as many as in the past... Anyone who missed Eileen Williams or Rick Breseman at the last few meets should not worry. They have been in Japan. Rick is back now, and Eileen should be back in May. Let us hope that the control descriptions at meets they go to in Japan use international symbols so they don't have to interpret specialized Japanese orienteering terms... As a change of pace, some of you may have already seen the new 4-wheel drive vehicle that Robert and Sandy Miller recently bought. That should help them get to meet sites... Thanks to the article about orienteering that Roger Sharp had published in the Seattle Times a few weeks ago, we got a lot of inquiries about orienteering and at least two new members. Jan Bjorup is a 19-year-old Swedish exchange student attending Roosevelt High School. He did well at the big meet at Tenalquot just after Thanksgiving. From Finland we have Matti Lopponen, who will be here for two years. He is a top orienteer whose specialty was the night leg of the Tiomila relay (this 100-kilometer relay in Sweden is probably the most prestigious in the world). He came in first at our low-key night-O meet on Halloween and also at the Tenalquot meet against experienced international competitors. Maybe he can teach us how he does it. Anyway, our warmest welcome to both Jan and Matti... Bergen, Norway resident and former COC member Stein Sandven spent two nights with Anne and me between work conferences. Stein was here in 1981-82, so not too many current members remember him, but he was the Foster Island map field-checker that winter and spring. Those who were at the Woodland Park meet were invited to supper with him and we all had an enjoyable time. One of the ideas we discussed at supper was to attend the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Carnival in Tasmania in January 1988. Anne and I attended meets in...

Page 3

Scotland and Wales with Stein and some of his friends from Bergen, and we can assure you that they know how to have a good time. Aussies are supposed to be the best people in the world to party with (just don't try to match them drink for drink), so with excellent terrain for orienteering and the splendid convivial atmosphere, that should be one fantastic orienteering vacation. I'm saving up money and vacation days already.... Rumor has it that COC will be ten years old in 1987. Perhaps if we plan a birthday meet for the summer of 1987, we may get some of those Aussies up here as well as other folks from back east....

Anyone with anything else you want to see in this column, let me know (births, goings, job changes, etc.) I still don't know everyone in the club yet, even though I try to.

SEE HOW THEY RODE (AND RAN)

On November 22, our club held a novel type of event: bus orienteering. Organized as a large-scale score event, it tested the ability of the participants to use city street maps and the Metro bus system to navigate around the city.

The location of each control was given as a business name and street address somewhere in Seattle. The object was to locate the control on the street map and figure out how to best get there using the bus system. The controls were displays in retail businesses, the "orienteering special of the day." To verify finding a control, each participant recorded the price of the special in addition to punching their control card.

The only allowed means of getting from one control point to the next were riding the bus and walking or running. There was a time limit of four hours, from the mass start at 10:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m., with a penalty of five points for each minute late. The total points possible was 500, requiring visiting all of the 19 controls.

The following is Dave Enger's account of his experience at the meet:

I arrived at Westlake Mall (4th & Pine, downtown Seattle) at about 8:45 am to register, socialize, and plan strategy. Copied the control locations onto my city street map, reviewed the control descriptions and event instructions, and perused the 40 bus schedules. Decided to start with the areas and bus routes with which I am most familiar. My planned route included Queen Anne, Ballard, and the University District, then the less familiar Central Area, Rainier Valley, Beacon Hill, and Georgetown. Realized that I probably would have to skip the West Seattle and far south end controls before picking up the close-by downtown ones.

Start 10:00 a.m. - Westlake Mall. Ran four blocks, about 0.3 mile, to Third Avenue and Virginia Street intending to catch bus #1 or #2. Bus #18 arrived immediately. Brief instant of panic until I saw that bus #18 also goes to the Seattle Center. Boarded bus feeling good about getting off to a fast start. Looked back to see Phil and Michelle Everett, who had boarded the same bus just one block from the Westlake Mall!

Page 4

Rode bus #18 0.9M to control #6. Waited about four minutes, then rode bus #2 1.0M to control #5. Helped the clerk find the control display, which had been stashed under the counter! Ran 1.1M down the north side of Queen Anne Hill to control #4. Boarded bus #17, which arrived immediately. My alternate plan was to run to Fremont to catch bus #28, then #43 to Ballard, but that would have been much slower.

Rode bus #17 2.3M to control #1. Waited about seven minutes, then rode bus #43 to control #2. The clerk, manager, and owner all said they had no O' control display. Used the restroom, then ran 1.5M to control #3. Waited about three minutes, then rode bus #43 3.7M to 11th Avenue East and East John Street. Ran 0.4M to control #7. My alternate plan was to ride bus #7, which would have taken me closer to the control, but bus #43 arrived first.

Waited about four minutes for bus #2, which was due. Became impatient and began running east on Union Street along the bus route. The bus never did show up, so I ran 1.3M to control #12.

Waited about seven minutes for bus #4 or bus #48, which weren't due for a few more minutes. Became impatient, changed my route plan, and ran 1.2M to control #11. Waited about two minutes, then rode bus #7 to control #15.

Now in less familiar territory, I sat down at the bus stop to plan my route. With about 75 minutes remaining, decided I didn't have time for control #19 in Rainier Beach, but should catch bus #48 to control #16 on Beacon Hill. While I was examining my schedules, a young woman sat down on the next bench. Since the bus stop sign was missing, I asked her whether bus #48 stopped there. She said yes, she just got off of it. I asked her whether I had been sitting there when she got off. She said yes again. I realized that while I had been reading the schedules, bus #48 had come and gone! After a ten minute wait, I rode bus #7 1.2M to Rainier Avenue South and South Alaska Street. Ran 0.8M up to control #16 at the top of Beacon Hill, which I wouldn't have had to do if I had caught bus #48!

Ran 0.4M south along Beacon Avenue South until I was overtaken by bus #31, which I rode 0.5M to South Graham Street. Ran 0.8M downhill and across Interstate 5 to control #17. I had no plan for getting from Georgetown to downtown in the last forty minutes. I was really lucky here. Waited about twelve minutes, then rode bus #123 4.1M to Fourth Avenue at Yesler Way downtown.

Ran 0.2M to the address listed in the description sheet for control #10. The control was not at that address. Greeted two other orienteers who also were looking for it. Since the self-confident orienteer knows when to move on, I abandoned the search and ran 0.3M to control #9. Clerk was impressed with the number of punches on my control card. Ran 0.3M to control #8. Ran 0.2M to the finish at Westlake Mall, by my watch arriving at 1:59:55. Five seconds to spare!

Summary:

total riding17.9 miles (9 buses)
total running8.8 miles (13 segments)
total distance26.7 miles (a marathon!)
total time waiting for buses49 minutes

I'm looking forward to next year's bus-O'!

BUS-O - November 22
Cascade Orienteering Club

Meet Director - Mike Schuh

I would like to thank the following people for their assistance in making this event happen: Pat Albright, Kurt Blakstad, Judy Cohen, Nancy Engel, Arnold Kay, Allan Griggs, Debbie Newell, and Roger Sharp, of COC; Heidi Stamm, Roger Helgerson, T.H. Peterson, and Dan Williams, all of Metro Transit; John Daniels and Larry of Captain's Nautical Instruments and, of course, a very warm THANK YOU to the 19 merchants who kindly consented to have the display stands ("control markers") in their businesses. I'd also like to give special thanks to one member of COC who, when I was considering ditching the entire enterprise, told me that it mattered if I kept working on it - at least to that member. Thank you one and all!

Mike Schuh

THE EVERGREEN O' CUP - November 29

Nisqually Orienteers

by Gary McCausland

On Saturday, November 29, the Nisqually Orienteering Club hosted the Evergreen 0' Cup. Eighty-four competitors participated on four different courses at the Tenalquot meet site on Fort Lewis.

Page 8

The event was preceded by a clinic at Yelm High School. Thirty-six people representing 7 area schools attended the clinic, which served as the kick-off event for the 1986-87 Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL). In addition to the high school attendees, there were a number of beginning and intermediate orienteers that used the clinic to learn some skills of the sport. The effort to host this clinic was made by Mike Tharp, with help by Bob Forbes, Carl Moore, Ron Pontius, and Fred Veler. Peggy Tharp worked hard at getting out publicity to the media and preparing the brochure for the WIOL. All their efforts were rewarded from the response and great comments.

At 12 o'clock the individual competition began. Because of the influx from the clinic, there was some confusion to start with. On behalf of the club, our apologies for any inconvenience. On all courses there was some good competition that added to the enjoyment of the event. Because of the complexity of the terrain, most competitors were challenged by the courses. Fortunately, the weather was cool and dry, not like last February when a blanket of snow covered the ground.

Many thanks go to those people that helped out with the administration of the meet. The instructors from the clinic continued to assist their students; Peggy and Mike Tharp acted as greeters; Ed and Elaine Rybak handled the registration; Mike Schuh came in and gave a hand with the starts before he was relieved by Peggy so he could confront the course; Kevin Hart was there at the finish to check times and punch cards. Of course, Meet Director Gary McCausland was in his typical state of perpetual confusion!

EVERGREEN O' CUP RELAY

by Mike Schuh

Prior to the individual event of the Evergreen O' Cup there was a relay event. Despite the small turnout, it was fun and even a bit exciting. Good weather helped as well.

The relay had three legs, that is, each team had three runners who ran in succession after a mass start. The course had a forking arrangement which prevents following, yet allows the competitors (and spectators!) to see how each team is doing.

The method used at Tenalquot was the "Farsta method," named after the Swedish town where it was first used. In this method, each runner's course consists of both common and forked controls (see map below). While each leg will be different, after the race is completed, each team will have covered the same ground, though perhaps in a different order from all other teams. The Farsta method is used in the World Orienteering Championships and the Swedish Tiomila race, among others.

[Diagram: Farsta method relay schematic showing branching paths ZL, ZM, ZK, etc.]

In the results below, note the close finish between Arnold Kay and Dave Enger and how close to Cascade's team Dale Width brought the Ellensburg team. The longest variation was 2205 meters, the shortest 2010 meters.

I'd like to thank Gary McCausland and Pat Albright for their help in organizing the event.

CLUB MEETING - JANUARY 28, 1987

COC's first club meeting of the year will be held on January 28 at the Green Lake branch of the Seattle Public Library (7364 E. Green Lake Dr. N.) at 7:00 p.m. Our primary business will be to elect a President, Vice President, and Secretary, since there were no candidates for these offices at our election meeting in November. Doug Sprugel was elected Treasurer at that meeting, Ardis Dull has agreed to run for Secretary at the January meeting, and Arnold Kay is a candidate for President, but we still need a candidate for Vice President. Anyone who is interested in any of these positions, please call Robert Miller (782-1437) or just show up at the meeting. Nominations will be accepted from the floor.

COC TO BID FOR 1988 WESTERN U.S. CHAMPS

At its December board meeting, the COC Board of Directors voted to bid for the 3rd Western U.S. Championships, to be held in 1988. If our bid is accepted, we have two possible locations for the meet: Marckworth Forest near Duvall, or Moran State Park on Orcas Island in the San Juans.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 JAN-FEB 1987 the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club

A strong contingent of 13 Puget Sounders travelled to the San Francisco Bay area for the class "A" California Championships meet hosted by the Bay Area Orienteering Club on October 18 and 19. What awaited us were some of the most physically brutal courses I've ever run.

The event was held at Briones Regional Park, located just west of Concord and Walnut Creek, in the hills east of San Francisco Bay. The area consists of steep, dry, half-open hills, typical of BAOC east bay areas. In fact, the area is so steep that the new 1:15,000 scale map uses a 10 meter contour interval rather than the usual 5 meters! The south-facing slopes are generally open, with a covering of dry grasses. The north-facing slopes are generally wooded, with deciduous trees, many "fight" areas, and abundant poison oak.

The courses were short, but this was more than made up for by the sometimes excessive amounts of climb. The blue course had about 500 meters of climb each day, and all but the white and Day 1 yellow courses exceeded the 4% climb limit set by the International Orienteering Federation, with climbs ranging up to 7%. One 700-meter leg on the Day 1 blue course had a 160-meter climb! Some of us experienced some difficulty adjusting to the 10-meter contour interval, which makes reentrants appear less deep and hills appear less steep on the map. The winning times on the advanced courses were around 100 minutes, much longer than the recommended 60-80 minute winning times.

The highlight of the northwest results was the clean sweep of the women's elite class (F21A) by Debbie Newell, Eileen Williams, and Mary Forbes. Other Puget Sound area participants were Rick Breseman, Dave Enger, Bob Forbes, Arnold Kay, Gary McCausland, Ron Pontius, Mike Schuh, Roger Sharp, and Doug and Katie Sprugel. Wayne Patterson from Portland, and Ross Burnett from Vancouver, B.C. also competed. Ross, a member of the Canadian National O' Team, won the Day 1 blue course despite having his shoe blow out at the 5th control!

The BAOC will be hosting the U.S. Long-O and Relay Championships next June on a new map of a redwood forest area west of San Jose. The meet will be the first weekend of a week-long series of orienteering events in California, including the U.S. Orienteering Federation convention and "A" meet in San Diego. Rumor has it that the BAOC Long-O and Relay area is virtually all wooded, has few man-made features, and has steeper and more detailed terrain than Briones! Since long-o courses are about twice as long as regular orienteering courses, the coming BAOC meet could be a real killer. I am planning to go, but I may run a course shorter than blue. I hope I'll have recovered from the Briones poison oak by then!
KNUT OLSON WINS M60 NATIONAL CHAMPIONSHIP

Knut Olson became COC's first national champion with his victory in the men's 60 and over division at the U.S. Orienteering Championships November 1-2 in Texas. Knut, one of Sweden's first orienteers, has lived in this country for over 35 years, and returned to the sport just a couple of years ago. Congratulations, Knut!
This column is back by popular demand (at least 5 people asked me to do it), so here it is again.

Special thanks to those half-dozen or so folks who volunteered space to lodge visiting orienteers during the Trollhaugen meet in September. we had space for 50 more people, but those who I didn't get back to weren't needed for that meet. Please step forward to offer your hospitality the next time a call for lodging volunteers is made... COC will be losing two active members soon. Don Denbo will be a research assistant professor in oceanography at Harvard University in Boston beginning in February, and Scott Coble will be a professor in mathematics and computer science at Gonzaga University in Spokane beginning in January. Don will be able to join the biggest club in the U.S., the New England Orienteering Club, where they put on two class "A" meets each year. Some of us will see him at the 1987 U.S. Championships in Rhode Island next October if not before. Scott will probably get to some COC meets, but not as many as in the past... Anyone who missed Eileen Williams or Rick Breseman at the last few meets should not worry. They have been in Japan. Rick is back now, and Eileen should be back in May. Let us hope that the control descriptions at meets they go to in Japan use international symbols so they don't have to interpret specialized Japanese orienteering terms... As a change of pace, some of you may have already seen the new 4-wheel drive vehicle that Robert and Sandy Miller recently bought. That should help them get to meet sites... Thanks to the article about orienteering that Roger Sharp had published in the Seattle Times a few weeks ago, we got a lot of inquiries about orienteering and at least two new members. Jan Bjorup is a 19-year-old Swedish exchange student attending Roosevelt High School. He did well at the big meet at Tenalquot just after Thanksgiving. From Finland we have Matti Lopponen, who will be here for two years. He is a top orienteer whose specialty was the night leg of the Tiomila relay (this 100-kilometer relay in Sweden is probably the most prestigious in the world). He came in first at our low-key night-O meet on Halloween and also at the Tenalquot meet against experienced international competitors. Maybe he can teach us how he does it. Anyway, our warmest welcome to both Jan and Matti... Bergen, Norway resident and former COC member Stein Sandven spent two nights with Anne and me between work conferences. Stein was here in 1981-82, so not too many current members remember him, but he was the Foster Island map field-checker that winter and spring. Those who were at the Woodland Park meet were invited to supper with him and we all had an enjoyable time. One of the ideas we discussed at supper was to attend the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Carnival in Tasmania in January 1988. Anne and I attended meets in...

Scotland and Wales with Stein and some of his friends from Bergen, and we can assure you that they know how to have a good time. Aussies are supposed to be the best people in the world to party with (just don't try to match them drink for drink), so with excellent terrain for orienteering and the splendid convivial atmosphere, that should be one fantastic orienteering vacation. I'm saving up money and vacation days already.... Rumor has it that COC will be ten years old in 1987. Perhaps if we plan a birthday meet for the summer of 1987, we may get some of those Aussies up here as well as other folks from back east....
Anyone with anything else you want to see in this column, let me know (births, goings, job changes, etc.) I still don't know everyone in the club yet, even though I try to.

On November 22, our club held a novel type of event: bus orienteering. Organized as a large-scale score event, it tested the ability of the participants to use city street maps and the Metro bus system to navigate around the city.

The location of each control was given as a business name and street address somewhere in Seattle. The object was to locate the control on the street map and figure out how to best get there using the bus system. The controls were displays in retail businesses, the "orienteering special of the day." To verify finding a control, each participant recorded the price of the special in addition to punching their control card.

The only allowed means of getting from one control point to the next were riding the bus and walking or running. There was a time limit of four hours, from the mass start at 10:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m., with a penalty of five points for each minute late. The total points possible was 500, requiring visiting all of the 19 controls.
The following is Dave Enger's account of his experience at the meet:

I arrived at Westlake Mall (4th & Pine, downtown Seattle) at about 8:45 am to register, socialize, and plan strategy. Copied the control locations onto my city street map, reviewed the control descriptions and event instructions, and perused the 40 bus schedules. Decided to start with the areas and bus routes with which I am most familiar. My planned route included Queen Anne, Ballard, and the University District, then the less familiar Central Area, Rainier Valley, Beacon Hill, and Georgetown. Realized that I probably would have to skip the West Seattle and far south end controls before picking up the close-by downtown ones.

Start 10:00 a.m. - Westlake Mall. Ran four blocks, about 0.3 mile, to Third Avenue and Virginia Street intending to catch bus #1 or #2. Bus #18 arrived immediately. Brief instant of panic until I saw that bus #18 also goes to the Seattle Center. Boarded bus feeling good about getting off to a fast start. Looked back to see Phil and Michelle Everett, who had boarded the same bus just one block from the Westlake Mall!

Rode bus #18 0.9M to control #6. Waited about four minutes, then rode bus #2 1.0M to control #5. Helped the clerk find the control display, which had been stashed under the counter! Ran 1.1M down the north side of Queen Anne Hill to control #4. Boarded bus #17, which arrived immediately. My alternate plan was to run to Fremont to catch bus #28, then #43 to Ballard, but that would have been much slower.

Rode bus #17 2.3M to control #1. Waited about seven minutes, then rode bus #43 to control #2. The clerk, manager, and owner all said they had no O' control display. Used the restroom, then ran 1.5M to control #3. Waited about three minutes, then rode bus #43 3.7M to 11th Avenue East and East John Street. Ran 0.4M to control #7. My alternate plan was to ride bus #7, which would have taken me closer to the control, but bus #43 arrived first.
Waited about four minutes for bus #2, which was due. Became impatient and began running east on Union Street along the bus route. The bus never did show up, so I ran 1.3M to control #12.

Waited about seven minutes for bus #4 or bus #48, which weren't due for a few more minutes. Became impatient, changed my route plan, and ran 1.2M to control #11. Waited about two minutes, then rode bus #7 to control #15.

Now in less familiar territory, I sat down at the bus stop to plan my route. With about 75 minutes remaining, decided I didn't have time for control #19 in Rainier Beach, but should catch bus #48 to control #16 on Beacon Hill. While I was examining my schedules, a young woman sat down on the next bench. Since the bus stop sign was missing, I asked her whether bus #48 stopped there. She said yes, she just got off of it. I asked her whether I had been sitting there when she got off. She said yes again. I realized that while I had been reading the schedules, bus #48 had come and gone! After a ten minute wait, I rode bus #7 1.2M to Rainier Avenue South and South Alaska Street. Ran 0.8M up to control #16 at the top of Beacon Hill, which I wouldn't have had to do if I had caught bus #48!

Ran 0.4M south along Beacon Avenue South until I was overtaken by bus #31, which I rode 0.5M to South Graham Street. Ran 0.8M downhill and across Interstate 5 to control #17. I had no plan for getting from Georgetown to downtown in the last forty minutes. I was really lucky here. Waited about twelve minutes, then rode bus #123 4.1M to Fourth Avenue at Yesler Way downtown.

Ran 0.2M to the address listed in the description sheet for control #10. The control was not at that address. Greeted two other orienteers who also were looking for it. Since the self-confident orienteer knows when to move on, I abandoned the search and ran 0.3M to control #9. Clerk was impressed with the number of punches on my control card. Ran 0.3M to control #8. Ran 0.2M to the finish at Westlake Mall, by my watch arriving at 1:59:55. Five seconds to spare!
I'm looking forward to next year's bus-O'!
[Map: Cascade Orienteering Club Seattle Bus Orienteering. The map shows Dave Enger's Route with stops numbered along a path through Seattle.]

Points: Start: 10:00 #6: 23 pts #5: 34 pts (Queen Anne) #4: 40 pts #1: 35 pts (Ballard) #2: 20 pts #3: 25 pts (University) #7: 25 pts #12: 35 pts #11: 8 pts #15: 19 pts #16: 35 pts (Beacon Hill) #17: 50 pts (Georgetown) ...and downtown points.

ROBINSWOOD PARK - November 16 Sammamish Orienteering Club

Meet Director - Dave Enger Registration - Dorothy Wendler Timing - Dave Tallent, Debbie Newell, Sandy Miller Television Spokesman - Robert Miller Marker Retrieval - the Daves (Enger, Lilly, and Tallent)
A cool, overcast day and television coverage by KOMO made for a pleasant meet.

I would like to thank the following people for their assistance in making this event happen: Pat Albright, Kurt Blakstad, Judy Cohen, Nancy Engel, Arnold Kay, Allan Griggs, Debbie Newell, and Roger Sharp, of COC; Heidi Stamm, Roger Helgerson, T.H. Peterson, and Dan Williams, all of Metro Transit; John Daniels and Larry of Captain's Nautical Instruments and, of course, a very warm THANK YOU to the 19 merchants who kindly consented to have the display stands ("control markers") in their businesses. I'd also like to give special thanks to one member of COC who, when I was considering ditching the entire enterprise, told me that it mattered if I kept working on it - at least to that member. Thank you one and all!
Both Roger Sharp and Bruce Terrell left early, Roger for some personal business and Bruce to visit a dentist. Maxine continued by herself.
On Saturday, November 29, the Nisqually Orienteering Club hosted the Evergreen 0' Cup. Eighty-four competitors participated on four different courses at the Tenalquot meet site on Fort Lewis.

The event was preceded by a clinic at Yelm High School. Thirty-six people representing 7 area schools attended the clinic, which served as the kick-off event for the 1986-87 Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL). In addition to the high school attendees, there were a number of beginning and intermediate orienteers that used the clinic to learn some skills of the sport. The effort to host this clinic was made by Mike Tharp, with help by Bob Forbes, Carl Moore, Ron Pontius, and Fred Veler. Peggy Tharp worked hard at getting out publicity to the media and preparing the brochure for the WIOL. All their efforts were rewarded from the response and great comments.

At 12 o'clock the individual competition began. Because of the influx from the clinic, there was some confusion to start with. On behalf of the club, our apologies for any inconvenience. On all courses there was some good competition that added to the enjoyment of the event. Because of the complexity of the terrain, most competitors were challenged by the courses. Fortunately, the weather was cool and dry, not like last February when a blanket of snow covered the ground.

Many thanks go to those people that helped out with the administration of the meet. The instructors from the clinic continued to assist their students; Peggy and Mike Tharp acted as greeters; Ed and Elaine Rybak handled the registration; Mike Schuh came in and gave a hand with the starts before he was relieved by Peggy so he could confront the course; Kevin Hart was there at the finish to check times and punch cards. Of course, Meet Director Gary McCausland was in his typical state of perpetual confusion!
(my apologies for not getting her time)

Club Key BEHS - Bellarmine High School BHS - Bethel High School COC - Cascade Orienteering Club EOC - Ellensburg Orienteering Club GHS - Garfield High School GVOC - Greater Vancouver O. Club NO - Nisqually Orienteers OO - Oregon Orienteers SOC - Sammamish Orienteering Club THS - Timberline High School WHS - Wilson High School YHS - Yelm High School
KELSEY CREEK PARK - December 6 Sammamish Orienteering Club

Meet Director - Dave Enger Course Designer - Bob Rein Registration - Dave Lilly High School Registration - Michelle Everett, Jennifer Seffernick Start Timing - George Lilly Finish Timing - Bob Rein Weather - cold & foggy
Prior to the individual event of the Evergreen O' Cup there was a relay event. Despite the small turnout, it was fun and even a bit exciting. Good weather helped as well.

The relay had three legs, that is, each team had three runners who ran in succession after a mass start. The course had a forking arrangement which prevents following, yet allows the competitors (and spectators!) to see how each team is doing.

The method used at Tenalquot was the "Farsta method," named after the Swedish town where it was first used. In this method, each runner's course consists of both common and forked controls (see map below). While each leg will be different, after the race is completed, each team will have covered the same ground, though perhaps in a different order from all other teams. The Farsta method is used in the World Orienteering Championships and the Swedish Tiomila race, among others.
[Diagram: Farsta method relay schematic showing branching paths ZL, ZM, ZK, etc.]

In the results below, note the close finish between Arnold Kay and Dave Enger and how close to Cascade's team Dale Width brought the Ellensburg team. The longest variation was 2205 meters, the shortest 2010 meters.
All of the participants agreed that we should have more relays in the region. Perhaps in time we can enter teams in the national relay championships.
I'd like to thank Gary McCausland and Pat Albright for their help in organizing the event.

A small but intrepid group of skiers ignored the rain in Seattle, and were rewarded with an overcast, but dry day at Trollhaugen. This was Knut's first time on cross-country skis in 40 years! Participants were treated to hot apple cider at the finish line.
ST. EDWARD STATE PARK - December 28 Cascade Orienteering Club

Meet Director - Arnold Kay Course Designers - Doug & Katie Sprugel Course Setters - Arnold Kay, Don Denbo Course Picker Uppers - Arnold Kay, Bernie Kay, Valerie Enger, Don Atkinson Registration - Don Atkinson Start Timing - Arnold Kay Finish Timing - Jim Siscel Beginners' Instruction - Rick Breseman Site Setup - Kurt Blakstad Pie Makers - Pat Albright, Don Denbo, Robert Miller, Mike Schuh, Anne York

COC's 7th annual Christmas meet lived up to it's "Bog Slog" nickname this year. Heavy rain fell most of the day, requiring participants to really work for the pies awarded to the first-place male and female on each course. The potluck and awards ceremony following the meet was, as always, an enjoyable way to see what everyone looked like dry.

Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Elizabeth Anderson, Bruce Cervene & Dyanne Kruger, James M. Davis, Richard Diesner, Ardis Dull, Alan & Jan Dutcher, Harvey Friedman & Anne York, Tom Hutchinson & Explorer Post 16, Ken & Katherine Schubert-Knapp, Laurie Kohli, Matti Lopponen, Michael Mahoney, Brad Payne, Bob Rein, and Jennifer Seffernick.
Members of Ellensburg and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs now receive Bearing 315 as a club benefit. These folks should renew through their own clubs rather than through COC.

Event Key: A - major event on good quality color map, 5 courses minimum B - major local event, 3-4 courses minimum C - local club event, 3-4 courses W - Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League meet (open to everyone) - change from last newsletter + - meet contingent on snow conditions - call before attending

COC's first club meeting of the year will be held on January 28 at the Green Lake branch of the Seattle Public Library (7364 E. Green Lake Dr. N.) at 7:00 p.m. Our primary business will be to elect a President, Vice President, and Secretary, since there were no candidates for these offices at our election meeting in November. Doug Sprugel was elected Treasurer at that meeting, Ardis Dull has agreed to run for Secretary at the January meeting, and Arnold Kay is a candidate for President, but we still need a candidate for Vice President. Anyone who is interested in any of these positions, please call Robert Miller (782-1437) or just show up at the meeting. Nominations will be accepted from the floor.
COC TO BID FOR 1988 WESTERN U.S. CHAMPS

At its December board meeting, the COC Board of Directors voted to bid for the 3rd Western U.S. Championships, to be held in 1988. If our bid is accepted, we have two possible locations for the meet: Marckworth Forest near Duvall, or Moran State Park on Orcas Island in the San Juans.
